title: Boil Pasta
summary: Boil pasta to the right texture and pair it with a simple sauce.
learnerPromise: Boil pasta to the right texture and pair it with a simple sauce.
whyItMatters: Pasta is an affordable, filling meal that stores well and cooks quickly. Knowing how to boil it properly means you can make a satisfying dinner with minimal ingredients.
realLifeUses: Making a quick dinner from pantry staples.
Feeding several people with one pot and simple ingredients.
Preparing a meal ahead of time for busy days.
Building a base for adding vegetables, protein, or leftover sauces.
youWillLearn: Boil water safely in a large pot.
Measure the right amount of pasta for one serving.
Test pasta for doneness before draining.
Combine pasta with a simple sauce and serve.
miniChallenge: Boil a pot of water and cook a serving of pasta. Test for doneness.
steps: Fill a large pot about two-thirds full with water and place it on the stove over high heat.
Add a small handful of salt once the water starts to simmer.
When the water reaches a rolling boil, add the pasta and stir gently to prevent sticking.
Set a timer for the minutes shown on the pasta package. Stir once or twice during cooking.
Before draining, taste one piece to check the texture. Drain the pasta, mix with sauce, and serve.
completionCriteria: The pasta is tender but still has a slight firmness when you bite it.
You drained the water and mixed the pasta with sauce without burning yourself.
commonProblems: The pot boils over — turn the heat down slightly and leave the lid off or partially on.
Pasta sticks together — stir it right after adding to the water and once more during cooking.
The pasta is too soft or too firm — adjust the cooking time by one or two minutes next time.
tips: Use a pot that gives the pasta room to move around.
Save a small cup of pasta water before draining — it helps sauce stick to the pasta.
Olive oil, butter, or a jar of ready-made sauce are simple ways to finish the dish.
helpBoundary: Ask for help if you are unsure how to use the stove. Turn pot handles inward so they do not get bumped.
